title: Preventing Iran’s Military Reconstitution
gist: Following 2026 U.S.-Israeli strikes that gutted Iran's navy, drone production, and missile facilities, Tehran will rush to reconstitute its military via foreign dual-use procurement. With its primary UAE reexport hub collapsing amid Iranian attacks on the Emirates, Iran is rerouting through Pakistan and the Caspian and leaning heavily on China, opening a narrow window for U.S./allied trade-control intervention.

themes.theme: Scale of damage to Iran's military

insights.claim: U.S. and Israeli strikes in 2026 destroyed nearly all of Iran's conventional naval surface fleet and partially blocked principal ports including Bandar Abbas with sunken vessels, halting bulk cargo offloading needed for reconstitution.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Port blockage is the binding constraint on Iran's entire rebuild; watch salvage activity and channel-clearing as the leading reconstitution indicator.
insights.locator: What Is Gone: Assessing the Damage

insights.claim: U.S. government assessments report Iran retains 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile but only 40 percent of its drone arsenal after expending an estimated 60 percent of drones and 30 percent of missiles in the conflict.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Drones are the more depleted and doctrine-central capability, signaling drone-component procurement will be Iran's most urgent and trackable demand.

insights.claim: Iran's missile and nuclear infrastructure is largely housed in hardened underground facilities, making the true extent of production-capacity damage publicly unknowable from open sources.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Surviving underground capacity means missile reconstitution may be faster and harder to verify than visible surface damage suggests.

themes.theme: Collapse of UAE reexport hub and new procurement routes

insights.claim: 95 percent of the UAE's direct non-oil exports to Iran in 2024 were reexports rather than UAE-produced goods, making the Emirates Iran's central laundering hub for foreign-origin dual-use items.
insights.speaker: CSIS
entities: UAE Ministry of Economy and Tourism
insights.so_what: The UAE channel was a transshipment conduit, not a production source; disrupting it forces Iran to expose new and less-developed routes.
insights.quote: 95 percent of the UAE's direct, non-oil exports to Iran in 2024 were reexports.
insights.locator: How Iran Will Seek to Procure New Trade Routes

insights.claim: Iran launched over 2,800 missiles and drones at the UAE between February and May 2026—more than at any other country including Israel—prompting the UAE to close its Tehran embassy and strike Iranian targets, likely ending the UAE-Iran trade relationship.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Iran self-destroyed its own top procurement channel; the collapse of UAE flows is the key inflection point creating the policy window.

insights.claim: Pakistan reportedly authorized reexports to Iran through its ports and territory as of April 25, 2026, offering a contiguous land route less exposed to U.S. or Israeli interdiction.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Watch third-country export increases to Pakistan with no domestic demand as an early indicator of Iran's rebalanced procurement architecture.

insights.claim: The Caspian Sea route via Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, and Turkmenistan into Iran's northern ports of Bandar Anzali and Amirabad is an attractive procurement pathway because it bypasses Persian Gulf chokepoints and is governed only by its five littoral states, making it hard to interdict.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Caspian transit lies outside U.S./allied maritime interdiction reach, so enforcement must shift to upstream third-country export monitoring.
insights.locator: Iran is also likely to develop more procurement pathways

themes.theme: China as supplier of first resort

insights.claim: China is the most likely supplier of first resort across all four of Iran's priority procurement categories—marine salvage equipment, machine tools, drone components, and marine engines—as Chinese goods are generally good enough even when Western versions are preferable.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: China's role is structural and broad-based; sanctions strategy must center on Chinese-origin and China-transiting flows rather than residual Western trade.

insights.claim: China leads global machine tool production at 36.9 percent in 2025 and exports at 21.6 percent in early 2026, and Iran's German CNC tool imports have already been largely replaced by Chinese imports.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The substitution toward Chinese machine tools is already underway, narrowing the leverage from Western export controls on finished tools.

insights.claim: Germany's Siemens and Japan's Fanuc dominate CNC machine tool controllers—the 'brain' of every CNC machine—with Fanuc on an estimated 50-60 percent and Siemens on 20-25 percent of CNC machines worldwide, and Chinese CNC machines including Anhui-based WMTCNC embed these same controllers.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The controller chokepoint is the highest-leverage disruption point; extraterritorial controls on Siemens/Fanuc content embedded in Chinese tools could choke Iran's high-end production.
insights.locator: However, Germany's Siemens and Japan's Fanuc dominate

insights.claim: Entities in China are a key source of Western-origin electronics for Iranian drone makers, but Iranian and Russian drone programs are increasingly using domestic Chinese components subject to U.S. foreign direct product rule controls.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The shift to domestic Chinese components erodes Western chokepoints but keeps items within reach of U.S. extraterritorial FDP rules.

insights.claim: The key gap among allied Iran sanctions regimes is extraterritoriality: U.S. controls follow the item via reexport and foreign direct product rules, while EU and Japanese controls generally stop at the border of the first export.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Closing this extraterritorial gap is the central policy recommendation; EU/Japan adopting U.S.-style reach is the structural fix for circumvention.
insights.locator: The main difference between the three sanctions regimes

insights.claim: Extending the EU's Russia-sanctions tools to Iran—chiefly a 'no reexport to Iran' contract clause and the anti-circumvention authority first used in April 2026 against Kyrgyzstan—is the recommended way to close gaps as UAE reexport networks migrate.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Russia playbook is a ready template; watch for EU adoption of a no-reexport-to-Iran clause as the signal that enforcement is tightening.

insights.claim: Direct trade between Iran and U.S. allies is already limited, with the EU exporting $4.3 billion in goods to Iran in 2024 and Japan only $89 million, meaning the procurement problem is overwhelmingly one of indirect transshipment rather than direct sales.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Enforcement value lies in third-country transshipment controls, not in cutting already-small direct allied trade flows.
insights.locator: There is already limited direct trade

insights.claim: Iran has over 40 years of experience operating worldwide covert acquisition networks, and Iranian distributor Technoset openly advertised import and clearance of sanctioned U.S., German, and Japanese goods on Instagram after EU sanctions were reimposed in December 2025.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Illicit networks are entrenched and brazen; expect increasingly complex multi-intermediary routing that raises costs and slows but does not stop procurement.
insights.quote: import and clearance of goods from the United States, Germany, and Japan . . . lifting sanctions
insights.locator: Iranian distributors have long had access

insights.claim: Iran claims far more imports of CNC controllers and machine tools from Turkey than Turkey reports sending to Iran, a sizeable discrepancy indicating concealed procurement routes via Turkish intermediary companies shipping from elsewhere.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Mirror-statistics gaps between importer and exporter reporting are an actionable analytical tool for detecting hidden transshipment.
insights.locator: Iran also reports significant imports of CNC machine tools from Russia

insights.claim: Iran's imports of high-powered gas turbines rose significantly from 2020 to 2025 shipped almost exclusively from Turkey, and marine compression-ignition engine imports rose roughly 500 percent from 2021 to 2025 coming almost exclusively from the UAE, with Iran being essentially the only destination for the UAE's large engine-component shipments.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Concentrated single-source flows with no plausible domestic demand are red flags; these specific commodity-route pairs are the highest-priority monitoring targets.

insights.claim: China's CAMA (Luoyang) Electromechanic, a military-civil fusion entity under AVIC, contracted to supply Iran with drone engine-testing dynamometers via the sanctioned Iranian company Sahara Thunder, illustrating direct Chinese state-linked support to Iran's drone program.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Chinese state-affiliated defense entities are directly enabling Iran's drone R&D, raising the stakes for diplomatic pressure on Beijing.

source_meta.author: Kate Koren, Patrick Panjeti, Emma Surnow, Kevin Kurland, Omar Al-Ghusbi
